attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-year history of progressive muscle weakness. She had poliomyelitis as a child affecting both legs but recovered with only mild residual weakness. She has noticed increasing difficulty climbing stairs and rising from chairs. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is asymmetric weakness of both lower limbs with muscle atrophy, more pronounced on the right. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the lower limbs.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following signs is most likely to be present on neurological examination of the lower limbs?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with post-polio syndrome, a condition characterised by new weakness occurring decades after acute poliovirus infection. The neurological examination findings reflect lower motor neurone dysfunction, as the primary pathology involves progressive degeneration of anterior horn cells and their enlarged motor units.

Fasciculations are spontaneous, visible muscle twitches that occur due to the spontaneous firing of motor units. They are a characteristic finding in conditions involving anterior horn cell or lower motor neurone dysfunction, including post-polio syndrome and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.

Babinski sign is an upper motor neurone sign indicating dysfunction of the corticospinal tract. It would not be expected in post-polio syndrome as the pathology primarily affects the lower motor neurons (i.e., the anterior horn of the spinal cord).

Ankle clonus is a series of rhythmic muscle contractions induced by sudden, sustained stretching of the tendon. Like Babinski sign, it is an upper motor neurone sign and would not be expected in post-polio syndrome.

Increased tone or spasticity is another upper motor neurone sign that results from loss of inhibitory signals from the cortex. Post-polio syndrome typically presents with decreased or normal tone due to its lower motor neurone pathology.

Hyperreflexia is also an upper motor neurone sign. In post-polio syndrome, reflexes are typically decreased or absent in affected muscle groups due to loss of lower motor neurones.

The distinction between upper and lower motor neurone signs is crucial in neurological diagnosis. Upper motor neurone lesions (affecting the corticospinal tract) produce spasticity, hyperreflexia, and Babinski sign. Lower motor neurone lesions (affecting anterior horn cells or peripheral nerves) produce fasciculations, atrophy, and hyporeflexia, as seen in post-polio syndrome.
learningPoints: Fasciculations are a characteristic finding in lower motor neurone diseases due to ongoing denervation of enlarged motor units
Lower motor neurone signs (fasciculations, atrophy, decreased reflexes) predominate in post-polio syndrome
references: Jubelt B, Agre JC. Characteristics and management of postpolio syndrome. JAMA. 2000;284(4):412-414. doi:10.1001/jama.284.4.412
Talley NJ, O'Connor S. Talley and O'Connor's Clinical Examination. 9th edition ed: Elsevier; 2021.
